And it's okay because we have a small army of volunteers ready to take the baton when work picks up/life gets in the way.

column30: I feel like our current Chicago DSA efforts/initiatives aren't having enough impact., The organizational processes and structure are difficult to navigate., I don't think the DSA is going to get anywhere without a full time staff. It also doesn't make much sense to me that an organization that fights for fair wages depends on volunteer work to run. Additionally, the excuse of everyone being volunteers is used a lot. Like you have the power to change the model of how you operate. By choosing to rely on volunteer work and expecting people to understand socialism in and out to join, the organization starts to feel like a cult.

column31:
The structure of CDSA is not conducive to members doing work or deciding what is important; it is the EC that is the defecto decision making body because branches are not empowered to act independently and GBM only happen once a quarter. Most decisions made in the chapter are done by the EC and the branches do not have the mandate to do their own work unless the entire chapter agrees once every three months. Which makes the chapter only act in reaction to the conditions in Chicago, we rarely if ever shape our conditions and touch our communities. 

The bureaucracy and structure of the chapter prevent members from being able to meaningfully engage with the chapter unless we are doing exactly what they want to do and even then if they don't make an infrequent meeting they miss out on participating. There is also very little outreach done so members don't know what we are doing and don't have any reason to engage with the chapter. We should be training our leaders to do listwork, and out reach after every meeting. Chapter leaders should be doing 1-on1s with new members/ all members in their branches. Branches should be doing political education so that when new members join we can develop them into socialists/deepen their politics so that they can then use their analysis to guide what we as a chapter should be doing to engage more of the working class.

column31:
It is very clear the chapter is immensely cliqueish with the main clique being in a political bubble. Extremism is rewarded extensively. Being a moderate is frowned upon. 

I genuinely found unless I agreed with a certain segment’s political whims I could never get anything done.

Mind how poorly organized the internals are.

I would love to rejoin, but only after the caucus clique lets new ideas actually take shape.
column32:
There were way too many meetings and socials. Like an excessive amount. And they were always at weird and inconvenient times.

I joined DSA to do political work. But it felt like a social club of people I genuinely despised or found vapid and boring. It was driving me bonkers to force myself to try and create friendships with people….I didn’t like being around. It’s ok to not be friends with everyone and focus on the work. But that’s the thing, it was never about the work. It felt like it was about being “a good leftist” like being in a specific social club.

Oh and do look into Alan Maass in education. He covered up a rape in his prior organization, to his own admission.

column32:
I mean... Just... The org itself is exhausting. There is a culture at a leadership level that "politics are nasty" and fighting dirty is "just part of the deal". Feminist Process is often lip service. I have seen officers of the org clap back on new members of color on the topic of race. I trust members of CDSA to want the same things I do, I don't trust them to operate in a spirit of transparency and collaboration to gain consensus on what is a correct outward facing action. I do trust members to incorporate beliefs across the leftist political spectrum into their considerations of my views, but I don't trust them to discuss real issues I have in good faith. We are at a precipice, I am looking for folks who have my back in the war against the fascist state. If we do nothing else as an org, we must build a culture of trust. That's much more difficult to do than remind people to "use I statements" at the top of every meeting. 


We have a long way to go and we are imperfect people working together imperfectly and it is exasperating. If we get it figured out that will be a huge step and a reward in itself, beyond the much more effective work we will become able to do. I believe in the democratic process of the org, but I worry about new members being able to find a home here. I have brought 2 former colleagues into the org and I routinely have to sit down and explain to them 'its not always like this'.

column32:
I think the chapter in general suffers a lot from its reactive approach to chapter-wide activities. We're only ever all called out to help someone else out with their protest, strike, etc. It's a bit disheartening to join the biggest socialist org in the US in the labor movement's home, just to find it hopping on bandwagons rather than actually leading. I've heard it's because we lack the active members to really act on our own in force, but that seems like a circular problem to me. How are people supposed to be inspired to join, participate, and feel part of something if we're only ever handing out water to someone else?

None of that is to take away from the committees' hard work. Some of the most inspiring and active socialists I've met were people who really knew their stuff and do the real daily work in those. I just think the chapter would be a lot healthier in general if that same energy were brought to chapter wide activities.

column32:
I like the goal of putting people first and bettering the lot of the working people . However, while I agree with your stances on abortion and LGBQT freedom and equality; they are not really issues that focus on the broader goals of income equality, equal access to medical services and education, housing, childcare, and expanding workers’ rights through unions, protests, and strikes. I feel if the latter were emphasized and the former deemphasized, you would increase your appeal to the typical working person.and therefore increase the likelihood of them becoming members. 
>>>I also realize that to be effective in any organization, you must sacrifice your personal views for the greater good of the organization.
I was an activist in my twenties and my affiliation with the DSA keeps me in my youth. 
I like being a member of the DSA. 
To the barricades !

column32: I had someone call me to recruit my attendance to a meeting. I said I have a child that makes it difficult to come to meetings. She told me DSA offers child care (which I already knew). I told her my kid is disabled and asked if the person in charge of child care was equipped to handle a special needs child. The tone of the conversation changed immediately and she was basically like "I'm just the person who makes the phone calls, I wouldn't know". Look. As a parent to a disabled kid I'm used to doors being shut in my face and spaces not being welcome to my family. I was really hoping DSA would not be one of those organizations. I get that she's just the person making phone calls but she could have told me she didn't know and she could connect me with someone else to continue the conversation. But instead she could not get rid of me fast enough. I'm too tired to have to fight to be accepted into a space that doesn't want me. I wish the best for DSA but ya'll need to work on actually being inclusive and remember who you're fighting for. The disabled community is one of the most powerless groups in our society.

column32: I've attempted to be part of progressive political community organizing groups in Chicago multiple times since 2013. I think what is missing is the genuine human connection that allows a new team member to feel like they belong. The general arc of my participation is: 1) attend a meeting, 2) the meeting asks people to get more involved through specific asks and implies that you're a bad person if you don't, 3) I volunteer for too many things, 4) back home alone with my work and life, I get overwhelmed by all these things I just volunteered for and in one way or another, don't do them, 5) I feel I can never show my face again and basically drop out. So is that just a me problem? Parts of it definitely are. But I wonder how common this pattern is. I also wonder if the whole thing could be derailed if a veteran member took me on as a buddy after my first meeting, we got to know each other, and that person became either a partner for doing those efforts or someone who would call me if I didn't show up next time. I was taught in IIRON community organizing training in 2012 or something that community organizers aren't your friends. But... I wonder if that is actually the problem. If we want to build strong communities, maybe we need to know each other as friends.

column31: I feel like the only other group that I would participate in other than Labor Branch regularly would be EWG but EWG meetings often get scheduled pretty last minute, aren't on the calendar always, are often on Zoom (which I really dislike for consequential decision-making), tend to not stick to a time limit particularly well or tend to go over time quite often, and sometiumes don't seem to have a clear agenda or moderation. Also, the vibe is often pretty unwelcoming with chairs sometimes acting frustrated about members having differing opinions than them or members kind of just using it as an opportunity to shit-talk decisions made by the EC or at GCMs. I wish that more EWG meetings were in person, regularly scheduled, kept to a more strict time limit, and that the chairs were better trained about how to make the meeting a place where participants felt welcomed even if they have disagreements on strategy with the chair. I'd like to be able to work EWG into a regular commitment but that's difficult to do when the day and time they get scheduled changes often and when its sometimes not clear what decisions are going to be made until very soon before a meeting begins.
column32: I would like to move away from the over-reliance on quickly scheduled zoom meetings. Even if they feel more accessible in some ways they also take the social part of organizing out of it and I actually think make it feel like Chicago DSA is something people should just slot in to their free time, which means that Chicago DSA meetings become less and less prioritized in people's lives. I think it would be healther if we prioritized regular, less frequent (even if that means slightly longer), and better organized in-person meetings to conduct non-urgent business. Obviously if some shit is popping off do what we gotta do, but regular branch or WG meetings should ideally be in-person.

column32:
I feel like I had to take significant initiative of my own to go to many meetings and discover what was going on across the org in order to plug in to work I felt effective doing. Even now that I'm relatively plugged in I feel like if I miss some meetings or stop looking at WhatsApp for a while I have to regain context by asking people I know in the org what's going on. 

Volunteer opportunities are presented at meetings without much context in the larger scope of the chapter. The strategic objectives of the chapter are defined by resolutions that I'm not even sure where I can look at them, I don't know how a new member would know what they are or how the ongoing work relates to them. The prioritization of work is also non-obvious.

column31: I'm old and tired and not in great health physically or mentally. I did enjoy being involved in comms work for the chapter a few years ago, but I often felt like I didn't fit in socially with the group and was put off at times by dogmatic and over-the-top attacks on Slack by some members on others (not me, thankfully). There wasn't a lot of this, really, but enough to make me cringe from time to time. My own politics have become -- how to put it? -- less orthodox than in the past, and while I do sense that some DSA members keep an open mind about the rapidly changing (and deteriorating) situation in the country and the world, much of DSA's public thought is still yoked to old slogans and categories that tend to constrain our analysis.
column32:
DSA's record of accomplishment in Chicago and nationally has been truly impressive. Reaching 100,000 members and getting socialists elected to public office in the USA is awe inspiring. If I can ever rouse myself from the physical and mental torpor I've been in for some time now, I may be back, certainly not for any leadership role, but to do whatever the 21st-century equivalent is of licking stamps and stuffing envelopes. 

By the way, this is a very well-designed survey. My compliments to the people who obviously put  a not of honest thought into creating it.

column32:
Several labor branch I attended were very cliquish. It's clear there is a group of friends who kind of dominate everything. I've gone several times with people I know and many of them felt the same way, and a number of them have not gone back because of it. I've gone back a few times and I do think it is noticeable but mostly I can't attend due to other obligations.

When I first joined I was surprised no one reached out to me. It was 2+ months before I got any official communication from CDSA. I had started trying to attend events on my own initiative at that point.

I also wanted to say that GCMs are too long. As a parent with a young child they are almost impossible to attend or participate in. CDSA is really structured around accomdating single, childless people from what I've seen.

column31: I generally feel out of place as a Black woman at DSA activities. I never feel like there’s a way for me to become part of the group or get involved in a way that goes beyond showing up to meetings and listening to other people. There’s a bro-y and condescending atmosphere that I find off putting, too — I am educated, and I even helped found a YDSA chapter at my college, but I have often had a feeling of being put in my place or talked down to at DSA events, by organizers and by other members, and a sense of being kind of socially and intellectually ignored because I’m not like, a white dude.
column32: I think the social weirdness I experience overall would feel less significant if it felt like there was some concrete task we were working on, rather than hanging out / talking / meeting / discussing always being so central. But I don’t know! Maybe it’s just the events that I’ve gone to? Maybe I don’t really get what DSA is supposed to be? But I want to participate and I’ve always felt sad that it just feels like not the right place for me, even though DSA aligns with my beliefs and political actions.

column32:
This chapter is immensely cliquish. Unless you are willing to subordinate yourself to the main east coast born clique, it is far too hard to accomplish anything. Hence why I quit. I have seen improvements but I will refrain from being a member until I see changes.

I also noticed an excess of social events and theory meetings but little actual work. Actually attempting work meets you on the wrong end of the clique who have a very specific idea of wha DSA is. To them it is all about unions, international issues and loudly being a socialist in name. There is no room for anything else.

column32: I never felt like CDSA was doing anything useful. I don’t know that i would have time for the book clubs anymore, but the ability to do something direct, meaningful, and political was so salient. column32: The infighting is a lot. I don’t know how to get around this, but it is a *huge* reason why I don’t want to participate. The caucuses, the factions, etc and then the personal attacks, it derails so much. I know this is endemic in left spaces, and the sheer volume of it is difficult to parse and also keep up with. i think we do need to be able to have principled disagreements and that is hard in a very big group that wants to move fast sometimes, and also hard when it seems like people are going to be consistently aligned with their group or caucus without room for experimentation or acknowledging they were wrong about something, etc. When I was involved, there was also a lot of shit talking about each other on the public internet. This also turned me off. Maybe that has changed I dont know, but something that wont' be solved in a day, and also something also to take seriously, I think.

column31: This is hard to say in a kind and constructive way, but I was just very put-off by the vibes of DSA-people and spaces. Everything was framed as very urgent and serious at chapter meetings, but it was often clear some of the most urgent/serious speakers were disconnected from political and practical realities. People seemed to claim DSA was accomplishing things all on its own rather than as a part of coalitions it was a part of. People seemed disinterested and uninformed about what people outside DSA thought. Many (not all, of course!) DSA members just seemed very online in a way I could not relate to or understand. I left because I have limited free time. Even though I felt more politically aligned with DSA than some other projects in the city, I didn't want to spend my limited time and social energy with those personalities. I now organize with a neighborhood group that has more eclectic politics that I sometimes feel uncomfortable with, but the people are so much nicer and well-adjusted (and also more knowledgable on specific local affairs). In short, I just found DSA annoying to be around. I felt like I was at a model UN club.

column31: Getting involved in leadership (steering committee) early on and feeling like I didn't have enough support which led to burnout. It was really hard to get other people to take on leadership roles or to depend on others to keep up with their leadership responsibilities. The people who were reliable were already way over committed so didn't feel like my efforts were sustainable or making an impact. I think there has to be a better pipeline for member --> political action event attendee --> taking on leadership role with continued support throughout. I think limiting branch and working group projects would make this more feasible so that people who are interested in taking action are funneled to the same projects rather than being spread across many projects. I think we should focus more on doing a few things well rather than doing a lot of things.
column32: I went to a political education event on multiracial organizing in 2024 where the white facilitator said they didn't need to self-reflect on how their implicit biases impact their organizing because they expected people of color to point out when they were acting on them. I've experienced sexist microaggressions (like I have in most spaces). I think given the values of democratic socialism, it would be helpful to promote strategies for helping members to reflect on their implicit and internalized biases and how they impact their behavior in the organization. Maybe in political education events?
